questions.prompt: You want to expose a service running on your home machine to the internet. Your ISP blocks port 80 and you don't have a public IP. Right tool?

choices.text: Ask the ISP for a static IP.

Cloudflare Tunnel. Install `cloudflared`, create a tunnel, route a DNS name to it, run the daemon. Outbound-only connection — works behind NAT, on residential networks, with ISP port-blocking. Five minutes of setup; no firewall changes needed. Combine with Access for identity-aware gating.

questions.explanation: Tunnel's killer feature is outbound-only — works through any NAT, firewall, or ISP restrictions. Five-minute setup; no public IP needed; secured by Cloudflare's network. Combine with Access (Lesson 5.1) for authenticated access.

questions.prompt: How do you achieve high availability for a Cloudflare Tunnel?

choices.text: Run multiple `cloudflared` instances on different servers, all using the same tunnel ID. Cloudflare load-balances across the connections automatically and fails over if one goes down. Same tunnel name, same credentials, multiple processes — that's HA.
Run two tunnels with different names.

questions.explanation: HA tunnels are built-in: multiple cloudflared instances connect to the same tunnel; Cloudflare load-balances. No special config required. Combine with systemd / process supervision for auto-restart on individual instances.
